---
title: "Practice-based Educational Data Science: A Proposal for Our Emerging Field"
subtitle: "A keynote presentation at the Stanford Education Data Science conference"
date: "9:30-10:15am, May 27"
author: "Joshua M. Rosenberg, Ph.D."
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    incremental: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
---

# Introduction

::: {.notes}
In this talk, Joshua Rosenberg examines whether educational data science is primarily a methodological program that takes education as a use case, or work situated in educational contexts and accountable to educational outcomes. 

Arguing that these paths lead to different kinds of work,  he will highlight key features of practice-based approaches

reflect on how the field can maintain rigor while better serving educators and learners in an era of AI.
:::

## The Problem

What is our field? This is meant to be a window and mirrow to the field - less about a study, more about the field.

I have a friend, and maybe you have a friend like this (or this person is you!) who is always interrogating and critiquing the subtle framing of research. If I say, for example, my goal is to improve educational outcomes, they will ask, of course:

which outcomes? A totally reasonable question. 

But then they will also ask, what do you mean by improve? A harder question.

And, even, what do you mean by "my goal"? At this point, I often found myself provoked and ready to abandon the conversation, but, on reflection, the questions are always thoughtful. 

This talk is meant (or should I say, I mean for this talk!) to get a bit at something related - what are the underlying goals of what we call educational data science? And, what worldviews underly and support (or undercut) these goals?

## Preopositions

Though I'm not usually _that_ person, I do have a bit of a history with this issue/question

![](/img/rmldt-1.png){width="70%"}

![](/img/rmldt-2.png){width="70%"}

## A proposition

But maybe that wasn't quite it --- it wasn't so much about method and pedagogy as about _in_ vs. _for_ education

So the argument is: Is EDS a methodological program that takes education as a use case, or is it work that is situated in educational contexts and accountable to its educational impacts? 

This talk argues the choice is impo and timely.

# Turn and talk

What brought you to EDS?

## My background

### Me!

![](/img/schoolpicture.bmp){width="70%"}

---

### My classroom!

![](/img/jr-classroom.jpg){width="70%"}

---

### Excel

![](/img/excel.jpg){width="70%"}

---

### Word Clouds

![](/img/word-cloud-fuglei.png){width="70%"}

::: {.notes}
I'll share very briefly where I am coming from. I was a science teacher, and I intended for that to be my career. I am still deeply tied to K-12 education and K-12 science education. I am PI for an NSF CAREER project that - broadly - supports science teachers to take up and to teach about data science ideas. My wife is a librarian working at a public school and so I engage, well, deeply through her and that work.

I came to data science through science education. I think of the spark being seeing students changing the colors of graphs they made in Excel, and seeing them relate to their product --- a small but meaningful relationship to data; claiming a relationship.

Another was seeing a colleague create an oft- (and I think correctly) bemoaned data visualization of text data, a word cloud. But they were so proud they created - coded - it. 

Still another was laying in bed in graduate school looking at R code I wrote, that worked. It was eye opening; I wasn't trained as a programmer, but I became, over time, interested, and then a bit obsessed, for years reading every book I could find (I think literally) on R, statistics using R.
:::

## CREDIBLE

STICKER

![](/img/credible-group.jpg)

![](/img/cld-bugs.png)

# Turn and talk

What brings you to the EDS conference?

# The Gaps and Problems

## Gaps

### The field is still niche

![alt](/img/eds-growth-abstract.png)

### There are a lot of unmet data needs

There is not yet a shortage of data related needs and challenges.

#### There is still a _lot_ of Excel.

#### There is a lot of interest on the part of educators and administrators

My survey findings.

Show interview data.

### Researchers' tooling is still not meeting the markk

Show data.

### Impact (finally?) matters

Consequence, impact, partnerships.

Not one-off engagement.

### Sharing in closed ways

Actually, other fields better here.

Edu not great (TCR).

Public goods.

## Problems

### Extant solutions are complex and hard to use

BB quote.

### Other fields have fundamental challenges (and strengths)

- Siemens and Baker (2012, *LAK 2*):

> "In education, the emergence of 'big data' through new extensive educational media, combined with advances in computation holds promise for improving learning processes in formal education, and beyond as well.
>
> Increasingly, very large data sets are available from students’ interactions with educational software and online learning - among other sources - with public data repositories supporting researchers in obtaining this data.
>
> Two distinct research communities, Educational Data Mining (EDM) and Learning Analytics and Knowledge (LAK), have developed in response."

- Baker and Yacef (2009, *JEDM*):

> "In recent years, advances in computing and information technologies have radically expanded the data available to researchers and professionals in a wide variety of domains, from government and biotechnology, to business logistics and professional sports.
>
> Likewise, recent increases in instrumented educational software, online courses, as well as databases of student test scores, have created large repositories of educational data. The Educational Data Mining community seeks to use these data repositories to better understand learners and learning, and to develop computational approaches that combine data and theory to transform practice to benefit learners."

- Long et al. (2011, *LAK 1*):

> "The idea for establishing a special dedicated forum to researching learning analytics was motivated by several important indicators:
>
> 1. The growth of data surpasses the ability of organizations to make sense of it. This concern is particularly pronounced in relation to knowledge, teaching, and learning.
>
> 2. Learning institutions and corporations make little use of the data learners "throw off" in the process of accessing learning materials, interacting with educators and peers, and creating new content.
>
> 3. In an age where educational institutions are under growing pressure to reduce costs and increase efficiency, analytics promises to be an important lens through which to view and plan for change at course and institutions levels."

### Funding is tight

Show NSF graphs, stories.

### Education is the focus of public attention and scrutiny

Show ed tech backlash stories, declining enrollment.

### Commercial tools predominate

XX.

### Other fields think they have it covered

IGSDSP.

### Oh, yeah, AI

It's a thing. More on that in a bit, though.

## In summary

Lots of gaps to fill; lots of problems to address!

| Gaps | Problems |
|---|---|
| The field is still niche | Extant solutions are complex and hard to use |
| There are a lot of unmet data needs | Other fields have fundamental challenges (and strengths) |
| There is still a _lot_ of Excel | Funding is tight |
| There is a lot of interest on the part of educators and administrators | Education is the focus of public attention and scrutiny |
| Researchers' tooling is still not meeting the markk | Commercial tools predominate |
| Impact (finally?) matters | Other fields think they have it covered |
| Sharing in closed ways | Oh, yeah, AI |

## Avenues

I don't plan to leave you hanging! There are things we can do!

## Right-sized problems

Iterate, make better

## Teacher education

- **Teacher preparation**: K-12 data science rollout is happening at scale, tens of thousands of teachers need curricula and PD, and the field has produced relatively little for them. Cite the numbers.

Cleveland mentioned! 

## Teacher support

Yeah

## Local evidence use

AskAboutEdu

## Quick insights

Transcripts of school boards

## Large scale policy analyses

gap!

## Non tech centric uses of data 

Quant and qual

## Modernizing quant methods

Lots of work to do here

## Ed admin and leaders prep

Data literacy

## Public goods and tools

- **Tools and partnerships for practitioners**: administrators, principals, and policymakers sitting on data they cannot use, with massive unmet demand and almost no supply from the field. Cite the numbers.

## A few words on AI (that haven't been said already)

 It can remove or engage us with practice

 More "AI as a normal technology", perhaps a more impactful one than many we have recently seen, perhaps with spikey and hard-to-anticipate impacts --- but not fundamentally different

 Repeatedly struck by how _not_ different AI coding is

    - Understand context and problem
    - Iterate (and iterate)
    - Check
    - Get feedback
    - Know the right direction to go

## The provocation

Back to the original question/tension --

Are we an education branch of "data science", or are we more integrally educational? 

Let's perspective take to try to answer. 

## Turn and talk 

If we were someone like Cleveland, what would we say?

If we were a parent, what would we say?

Is there any gap between them?

## Infrastructure 

We need to continue the conversation

This conference, here and now

Critical question: XX (back to original framing)

## In closing

We can do rigorous and impactful work in practice

Probably less of it

We can build a field that fills gaps and addresses problems

<!-- ===================================================================
IMAGE LIBRARY — parking lot

Every image in /img/ is listed below as ready-to-paste code. To use one,
cut a line out of this comment block and drop it onto a slide. Adjust the
width as needed (or delete `{width="..."}` for the image's natural size).

--- CLD / CREDIBLE project ---
![](/img/credible-group.jpg){width="70%"}
![](/img/cld-class.jpg){width="70%"}
![](/img/cld-class-bug.jpg){width="70%"}
![](/img/cld-group.jpg){width="70%"}
![](/img/cld-full.png){width="70%"}
![](/img/cld-screens.png){width="80%"}
![](/img/cld-query.png){width="80%"}
![](/img/cld-bugs.png){width="45%"}

--- Data tools (CODAP, spreadsheets) ---
![](/img/get-data.png){width="55%"}
![](/img/codap-ag-data.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/codap-bar.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/art-and-data.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/messy-data.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/messy-desktop.png){width="80%"}

--- Classrooms & teaching ---
![](/img/science-classroom.jpg){width="70%"}
![](/img/notebook-and-laptop.jpeg){width="55%"}
![](/img/coach-students.jpeg){width="55%"}

--- Teacher professional development ---
![](/img/pd-card-sort.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/pd-group-work.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/pd-laptop-diagram.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/pd-outdoor-pair.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/krebs-and-mertz.jpeg){width="70%"}
![](/img/utk-composting-sign.jpeg){width="45%"}

--- EDS field & literature ---
![](/img/eds-growth-abstract.png){width="80%"}
![](/img/eds-growth-thesaurus.png){width="80%"}
![](/img/cleveland-action-plan.png){width="55%"}
![](/img/cleveland-pedagogy.png){width="55%"}
![](/img/eds-past-present-future.png){width="80%"}
![](/img/eds-work-abstract.png){width="60%"}
![](/img/eds-skills-chart.png){width="85%"}
![](/img/software-use-table.png){width="85%"}
![](/img/dsieur-cover.jpg){width="35%"}

--- tidyLPA ---
![](/img/tidylpa-paper.png){width="65%"}
![](/img/tidylpa-paper-sidebar.png){width="75%"}
![](/img/tidylpa-website.png){width="75%"}

--- Sentiment & data-ethics research ---
![](/img/sentiment-model-eq.png){width="45%"}
![](/img/sentiment-variables-table.png){width="80%"}
![](/img/sentiment-results-table.png){width="80%"}
![](/img/public-sentiment-paper.png){width="65%"}
![](/img/facebook-ethics-paper.png){width="70%"}
![](/img/intimidation-table.png){width="70%"}
==================================================================== -->
